rationale: I chose 14 subspecialties to balance breadth (covering major practitioner identities) with pickability on a single grid. The list mixes discipline-based (Organic, Inorganic, Physical), technique/approach (Analytical, Spectroscopy, Computational), application-driven (Medicinal, Environmental, Polymer), and cross-cutting specialties (Catalysis, Organometallic, Electrochemistry, Supramolecular, Synthetic Methods) so most working chemists will see a single best fitting label. I deliberately did not create many microscopic splits (e.g., separate vibrational vs. NMR spectroscopists) to avoid overly thin categories, nor did I fold chemistry interfacing strongly with other Level‑1 categories (solid-state/electronic materials, biology-focused chemical biology) into this list — those go to Materials Science or Biology. A few borderline splits (Synthetic Methods vs. Organic Chemistry; Spectroscopy vs. Analytical Chemistry) were resolved by prioritizing how researchers self-identify in papers and lab groups: method-focused groups listed as “Synthetic Chemistry” or “Methodology,” whereas instrument/measurement-focused groups often call themselves “Analytical” or “Spectroscopy.”

subspecialties.name: Organic Chemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Structure, mechanism and synthesis of carbon‑based molecules
covers: reaction mechanisms, total synthesis, functional group transformations
examples: natural product synthesis, stereoselective methods, C–H functionalization
explicitly_excludes: Medicinal Chemistry — drug discovery/ADME-focused work
Synthetic Methods — methodology papers focused on general reaction platforms

subspecialties.name: Physical Chemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Thermodynamics, kinetics, spectroscopy, and molecular-scale theory
covers: chemical kinetics, statistical thermodynamics, surface dynamics, ultrafast processes
examples: reaction dynamics, phase behavior, charge‑transfer studies
explicitly_excludes: Computational Chemistry — large-scale modeling/simulation focused work
Spectroscopy — instrument- or technique-centric spectroscopy papers

subspecialties.name: Inorganic Chemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Spectroscopy, bonding and reactivity of non‑organic elements and complexes
covers: coordination chemistry, main‑group and transition‑metal compounds, solid complexes (non‑electronic materials)
examples: new metal complexes, cluster chemistry, main‑group reactivity
explicitly_excludes: Materials Chemistry/Materials Science — solid‑state electronic materials and device-focused work
Organometallic Chemistry — when the practitioner identity is organometallic

subspecialties.name: Analytical Chemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Measurement science, methods and instrumentation for chemical analysis
covers: methods for quantification, sample preparation, chromatography and hyphenated techniques
examples: method validation, sensors, mass spectrometry workflows
explicitly_excludes: Spectroscopy — fundamental spectroscopy technique development when authors self‑identify as spectroscopists
Environmental Chemistry — application of analytical methods to environmental monitoring

subspecialties.name: Spectroscopy
subspecialties.tagline: Development and application of spectroscopic and instrument techniques
covers: NMR, IR, Raman, UV‑Vis, EPR, X‑ray spectroscopy, and instrument development
examples: new pulse sequences, single‑molecule fluorescence setups, synchrotron techniques
explicitly_excludes: Analytical Chemistry — measurement‑focused method papers that position themselves as analytical
Physical Chemistry — spectroscopy used purely for physical dynamics interpretation

subspecialties.name: Computational Chemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Modeling, simulation and theoretical chemistry using computation
covers: quantum chemistry, molecular dynamics, cheminformatics and force‑field development
examples: DFT studies, free‑energy simulations, machine‑learning potentials
explicitly_excludes: Physical Chemistry — theory-driven experimental physical chemistry papers
Medicinal Chemistry — computational work specifically for drug design projects

subspecialties.name: Catalysis
subspecialties.tagline: Homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysis, mechanism and catalyst design
covers: catalyst discovery, mechanistic studies, turnover optimization, and scale‑up considerations
examples: supported catalysts, organocatalysis, electrocatalysis (if primarily catalyst‑focused)
explicitly_excludes: Electrochemistry — electrochemical mechanism/device papers where electrode processes are the focus
Organometallic Chemistry — catalyst ligand design when framed as organometallic science

subspecialties.name: Organometallic Chemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Chemistry of metal–carbon bonds and metal complex reactivity
covers: synthesis and reactivity of metal–carbon containing species, catalyst precursors
examples: metal carbenes, metal–ligand cooperative systems, bond activation
explicitly_excludes: Catalysis — applications of organometallic complexes as catalysts that are presented as catalysis work
Inorganic Chemistry — non‑carbon‑based inorganic work

subspecialties.name: Polymer Chemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Synthesis, characterization and properties of polymers and macromolecules
covers: polymerization methods, macromolecular architecture, characterization and properties
examples: block copolymers, stimuli‑responsive polymers, polymerization catalysis
explicitly_excludes: Materials Chemistry/Materials Science — device or bulk materials engineering and solid‑state electronics
Analytical Chemistry — polymer analysis papers framed as analytical method development

subspecialties.name: Electrochemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Redox chemistry, batteries, electrocatalysis and electroanalytical methods
covers: fundamental electrode processes, battery chemistry, electrocatalysis, and sensors
examples: Li‑ion battery chemistry, CO2 electroreduction, voltammetry technique development
explicitly_excludes: Materials Chemistry/Materials Science — electrode materials and device engineering when primarily materials-driven
Catalysis — non‑electrochemical catalysis work

subspecialties.name: Supramolecular Chemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Noncovalent assemblies, host–guest systems and molecular self‑assembly
covers: self‑assembly, host–guest chemistry, molecular recognition, stimuli‑responsive assemblies
examples: rotaxanes, host–guest sensing, dynamic covalent systems (when focused on noncovalent design)
explicitly_excludes: Polymer Chemistry — self‑assembled polymer systems when practitioner identity is polymer science
Chemical Biology/Biology — biological receptor/biomolecular recognition studies

subspecialties.name: Medicinal Chemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Small‑molecule design, optimization and preclinical chemistry for drug discovery
covers: lead optimization, SAR, ADME/PK chemistry, and chemical probes
examples: synthesis of drug candidates, metabolic stability studies, bioisosteric replacements
explicitly_excludes: Biology/Chemical Biology — biology-led mechanism-of-action studies and in vivo biology
Computational Chemistry — in silico ADME or docking when presented principally as computational work

subspecialties.name: Environmental Chemistry
subspecialties.tagline: Fate, transport and chemical impacts in the environment and remediation
covers: pollutant fate, atmospheric chemistry, remediation chemistry, and ecotoxicology‑related measurements
examples: microplastics chemistry, pollutant degradation pathways, water treatment chemistry
explicitly_excludes: Analytical Chemistry — purely method development papers for environmental sensing framed as analytical
Biology — ecological/organism-level impact studies that belong to Biology

subspecialties.name: Synthetic Methods
subspecialties.tagline: General reaction platforms, reagents and methodology development
covers: new reaction development, reagent design, and broadly applicable synthetic toolkits
examples: new cross‑coupling methods, reagent platform papers, scalable synthetic protocols
explicitly_excludes: Organic Chemistry — project‑specific synthetic applications or total synthesis papers
Catalysis — method papers framed primarily as catalysis rather than general synthetic tools

uncovered_concerns: Chemical Biology was considered but routed to Biology because many papers are biology‑centric (mechanism in cells, biomolecular targets) and practitioners identify as chemical biologists or biochemists.
Materials Chemistry / solid‑state and electronic materials intentionally excluded — those belong to Materials Science to avoid conflating device/materials engineering with core chemical science.

Spectroscopy vs. Analytical split is borderline: some groups identify as spectroscopists while others position the work as analytical method development; I split them based on author identity and framing.
Synthetic Methods overlaps with Organic Chemistry and Catalysis; I kept it separate for groups that present broadly applicable methodology rather than target‑oriented synthesis.

Biomolecular/biochemistry work that focuses on enzymology or metabolic chemistry was debated — those that emphasize biological function should go to Biology, whereas purely chemical mechanistic enzymology papers that identify as chemistry remain in Chemistry.
